cards.front: What is a class in Python?
cards.back: A blueprint or template that defines what data (attributes) and actions (methods) objects will have. You create objects (instances) from a class.
A concrete realization of a class. Each object has its own copy of instance variables but shares methods defined in the class.

cards.front: What is __init__ and when is it called?
cards.back: __init__ is the constructor method. It runs automatically when you create a new object, initializing the object's attributes.
A reference to the current object (instance). Every instance method's first parameter is self, which lets you access that specific object's attributes and methods.
A variable that belongs to a specific object. Created with self in __init__, each object has its own copy. Example: self.name = 'Alice'

cards.front: What does the __ (double underscore) prefix do?
cards.back: Triggers name mangling to make a variable private. Python renames self.__value to _ClassName__value, preventing accidental access from outside the class.
Turns a method into a getter that can be accessed like an attribute. Instead of obj.get_name(), you write obj.name without parentheses.
Defines a setter method that runs when you assign a value to a property. Used for validation before storing data. Example: @name.setter validates before assigning.
Defines how an object is printed as a string. Called automatically by print(obj) or str(obj). Must return a string representation.
A variable defined inside the class but outside any method. Shared by all instances. Example: species = 'Homo sapiens' is shared by all Person objects.
Bundling data (attributes) and methods together, hiding internal details with private variables and exposing only a clean public interface.
The process of creating an object from a class. Example: dog = Dog('Buddy') creates an instance of the Dog class.
